coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent nanoprobes based on D-cys-based CDs and quantitative detection of lysine enantiomers
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jinqiu
Wang, Fanghao
Zhang, Hao
Cao, Duxia
Guan, Ruifang
coredata.dc:description: The recognition of amino acid enantiomers is of great significance in drug research and disease diagnosis. However, the similar physical and chemical properties of enantiomers make chiral recognition challenging. Optical characterization is a promising approach to distinguish enantiomers. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention due to their simple synthesis, high economic benefits, and good biocompatibility. Chiral carbon dots, as fluorescent probes, have gradually received more attraction. Here, a fluorescence probe (CYS-CDs + Cu2+) was constructed using D-Cys as chiral source to synthesize chiral carbon dots (CYS-CDs), and Cu2+ as inducer to recognize the chirality of lysine enantiomers(L-/D-Lys). And then, based on the ability of Cu2+ to quench the fluorescence of CYS-CDs and L-Lysine (L-Lys) could restore the fluorescence of the CYS-CDs + Cu2+ probe, so achieve an “on–off–on” detection mode to detect L-Lys. On the contrary, D-Lys cannot restore the fluorescence of CYS-CDs + Cu2+. The probe can distinguish lysine enantiomers not only by fluorescence spectra but also by circular dichroism spectra. And, the probe can be used for the quantitative detection of L-Lys by fluorescence method, of which the detection range is 0–520 μM, and the limit of detection is only 13.70 μM. In addition, a paper-based fluorescent senor was constructed on this basis. This work may be of practical significance to expand the recognition method of amino acid enantiomers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral recognition
L-/D-lysine
Enantiomeric differentiation
Paper-based sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of the fluorescence probe (CYS-CDs + Cu2+)
Specific response of CYS-CDs to Cu2+
Application of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ in paper-based sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CYS-CDs (insert image represent the size distribution); (b) HRTEM image of CYS-CDs, Lattice spacing: 0.21 nm; (c) FT-IR spectra of CYS-CDs; (d) XPS survey of CYS-CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of CYS-CDs, (e) C 1s, (f) N 1s, (g) O 1s, (h) S 2p.

(a) The absorption (black line), excitation (blue line)and emission (red line) spectra of CYS-CDs. Inset: the optical images of G-CDs under daylight (left) and ultraviolet lamp (right). (b) FL spectra of CYS-CDs at different excitation wavelength from 305 nm to 405 nm. (c) Time-resolved spectroscopy of CYS-CDs. (d) Circular dichroism spectra of D-Cys (black line) and CYS-CDs (blue line).

The relative fluorescence intensity of CYS-CDs induced by common. (a) chiral substance and (b) ions (80 µM). Fluorescence intensity of the CCDs changes with (c) Sn4+ concentration (0–200 µM)/ (e) Cu2+ concentration (0–400 µM); the linear relationship between the fluorescence changes of CYS-CDs vs. (d) Sn4+ concentration (0–200 µM)/ (f) Cu2+ concentration (40–360 µM).

Fluorescence intensity of the CYS-CDs + Cu2+ change with (a) L-Lys (0–520 µM)/(c) D-Lys (0–520 µM) concentration; (b) the linear relationship between the fluorescence changes of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ vs. L-Lys concentration (0–520 µM).

Fluorescence intensity of the CYS-CDs change with (a) L-Lys (0–520 µM)/ (c) D-Lys (0–520 µM) concentration; the relationship between the fluorescence changes of CYS-CDs vs. (b) L-Lys concentration (0–520 µM)/ (d) D-Lys concentration (0–520 µM).
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ in the presence of different lysine ee%; (b) the relationship between the fluorescence peak intensity of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ vs. lysine ee%.

(a) Optical images of probe-treated paper（1: CYS-CDs, 2–6: CYS-CDs + Cu2+） with different concentrations of L-Lys (3–6: 50 µM、100 µM、150 µM、200 µM) added under daylight and ultraviolet lamp； /(b) histogram of the changes of the RGB of the three primary color light modes;(c) The line diagram of G and B value changes of samples in groups 2–6 is the linear fitting diagram of G value and L-Lys concentration.

(a) Absorption spectra of the Cu2+ (black), CYS-CDs (red), CYS-CDs + Cu2+ (blue); Time-resolved fluorescence decays of (b) CYS-CDs/ (c) CYS-CDs + Cu2+; (d) CD spectra of the CYS-CDs (black), CYS-CDs + Cu2+ (purple), CYS-CDs + Cu2++L-Lys (cyan) and CYS-CDs + Cu2++D-Lys (green).
Concentration of lysine enantiomers and enantiomeric excess ee%.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of fluorescent nanoprobes based on D-cys-based CDs and quantitative detection of lysine enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science & Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for fabricating chiral CDs-based nanoprobes for assaying Cu2+ and L-Lys in On–Off–On mode.

The recognition of amino acid enantiomers is of great significance in drug research and disease diagnosis. However, the similar physical and chemical properties of enantiomers make chiral recognition challenging. Optical characterization is a promising approach to distinguish enantiomers. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention due to their simple synthesis, high economic benefits, and good biocompatibility. Chiral carbon dots, as fluorescent probes, have gradually received more attraction. Here, a fluorescence probe (CYS-CDs + Cu2+) was constructed using D-Cys as chiral source to synthesize chiral carbon dots (CYS-CDs), and Cu2+ as inducer to recognize the chirality of lysine enantiomers(L-/D-Lys). And then, based on the ability of Cu2+ to quench the fluorescence of CYS-CDs and L-Lysine (L-Lys) could restore the fluorescence of the CYS-CDs + Cu2+ probe, so achieve an “on–off–on” detection mode to detect L-Lys. On the contrary, D-Lys cannot restore the fluorescence of CYS-CDs + Cu2+. The probe can distinguish lysine enantiomers not only by fluorescence spectra but also by circular dichroism spectra. And, the probe can be used for the quantitative detection of L-Lys by fluorescence method, of which the detection range is 0–520 μM, and the limit of detection is only 13.70 μM. In addition, a paper-based fluorescent senor was constructed on this basis. This work may be of practical significance to expand the recognition method of amino acid enantiomers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In nature, the enantiomers of chiral molecules often play different or even opposite roles [1–3]. Although sharing the same chemical structure, chiral amino acid enantiomers demonstrate significant differences in pharmacology, toxicology, metabolism, and other aspects due to their distinct interactions with proteins or receptors within biological systems [4–7]. The recognition of chiral molecular enantiomers holds not only of great significance and application value in fields such as optical resolution, drug development, and stereoselective synthesis [8–10], but also facilitates a deeper understanding of many life processes by humans [11,12].

In general, L-type amino acids participate in various crucial life activities within the human body, while D-type amino acids are usually closely associated with metabolic abnormalities and diseases [13–16]. L-lysine plays an important role in the human body, aiding in calcium absorption, muscle protein development and the synthesis of hormones, enzymes and antibodies. However, metabolic disorder involving L-Lysine will lead to endocrine disruptions, impacting liver and kidney function, growth and development, etc. Given these serious implications, to establish an effective strategy for lysine enantiomer recognition is highly meaningful. Since most of the current technologies for identifying lysine enantiomers focus on electrochemical methods [17], gas chromatography [18], or the method using novel Binaphthyl Amine fluorescent probes [19], these technologies usually have various drawbacks such as high cost, complex synthesis, poor biocompatibility, and high toxicity. Therefore, it is imperative to develop simple and efficient methods for identifying L-Lysine and D-Lysine. Fluorescent probe-based methods are currently favored by researchers due to the ease of operation and environmentally friendly. In recent years, fluorescent chiral carbon dots (CCDs) have emerged as a new class of carbon nanomaterials, exhibiting excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, and low cost [20–22]. So far, the construction of fluorescent probes by combining fluorescent quantum dots with metal ions has received extensive attention [23–27], opening up unlimited possibilities for applications such as chiral catalysis, chiral synthesis, and enantiomer recognition and identification [28–31]. Cysteine, exhibits strong physiological activity and is greener and safer as a chiral source, providing the potential for subsequent products to possess high biocompatibility [32,33].

In previous studies, our group constructed chiral fluorescent probes by a two-step method [8] but faced complex synthesis and low yields. To simplify, we explored a one-step synthesis using polyethyleneimine (PEI), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), and D-cysteine (D-Cys), yielding chiral fluorescent carbon dots (CYS-CDs). Polyethyleneimine (PEI) has been proved its strong fluorescence capabilities under suitable conditions [34,35], attracting the attention of many researchers. Ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), as an excellent metal ion chelator [36,37], provides the potential for subsequent metal ion identification. The synthesis was conducted at a temperature below D-Cys's melting point to preserve the chiral structure.

Cu2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of CYS-CDs. L-Lys combines with CYS-CDs, preventing the collision of Cu2+, thus restoring the fluorescence and enabling the detection of L-Lys. However, D-Lys has no significant effect on the fluorescence, allowing distinction between lysine enantiomers. Finally, a paper-based sensor is constructed by utilizing the probe’s quantitative detection of L-Lys, intuitively indicating concentration through RGB value changes. This mechanism highlights the unique interaction between Cu2+, L-Lys, and CYS-CDs, leading to the development of a sensor that specifically detects L-Lys, distinguishing it from D-Lys. This research contributes to the field of chiral fluorescent probes and their applications.

Ethylene Diamine Tetraacetic Acid (EDTA), Polyethyleneimine (PEI) and all the amino acids (L-cystine, D-cystine, D-aspartic acid, D-isoascorbic acid, D-lysine, D-tryptophan, dopamine, glutathione, L-ascorbic acid, L-arginine, L-histidine, L-lysine, L-methionine, L-serine, L-threonine, L-tryptophan, L-valine) used in this experiment were from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The ions used in this experiment are derived from NaCl, CuCl2, CaCl2, AlCl3, NaI, KCl, Na2CO3 and so on. They are all from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals used are analytical grade and do not require further refinement.

Test methods and instruments are as follows: UV–Vis absorption spectra (Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer). Fluorescence spectra (Techcomp FS5C Spectrofluorometer). Time-resolved luminescence decay curve (Fluorocube-01, JY-IBH, Horiba). Absolute fluorescence quantum yield). TEM (JEM-2100F). Circular dichroism spectrum (ChirascanqCD). X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+). Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (Nicolet 380).

The synthesis procedure for CYS-CDs (D-Cys-based CDs) is illustrated in Scheme 1 . The process involves a one-step hydrothermal method. First, 0.50 g (0.83 mmol) PEI, 0.73 g (2.50 mmol) EDTA and 0.40 g (3.30 mmol) D-Cys were successively dispersed in 20 mL of ultrapure water, Then, the mixed solution was put into a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 160°C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm filter membrane and dialyzed for 24 h (MW = 1000 Da) to obtain the CYS-CDs solution. Finally, the resulting product was freeze-dried and stored at 4℃ for future use. As a comparison, L-CYS-CDs were prepared using L-Cys as a chiral source under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the fluorescence probe (CYS-CDs + Cu2+)
ce:section.ce:para:
All spectral acquisition procedures were performed in an aqueous solution at room temperature and pH 6.42 with the obtained CYS-CDs solution (0.5 mg mL−1), the fluorescent slit width is 2.00 nm bandpass. The selectivity of CCDs to Cu2+ was screened by monitoring fluorescent changes after adding common left-handed substances (L-tartaric acid, L-arginine, L-ascorbic acid, L-lysine, L-malic acid, L-tryptophan, L-aspartic acid, 80 µM) and common ions (Ca2+, Cd2+, ClO−, Co2+, CO3 2–, Cu2+, F−, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, I−, K+, Na+, Cl−, PO4 3−, SCN−, Sn4+, 80 µM) to CCDs. The metal ions are all from their corresponding hydrochloride solutions, the initial concentration of common left-handed substances and hydrochloride solutions used above is 1 × 10−2 M. First, 2.5 mL CCDs solution was added to a quartz cuvette, add 80 µM solution of the analyte therein dropwise, shake it up and stand for 2 min, then record the fluorescence spectrum (Ex = 345 nm). For all samples, the fluorescence intensity corresponding to the maximum emission peak was selected for quantitative analysis. The reaction time was selected based on the results shown in the Fig. S1
a, b.

The morphology and structure of CYS-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in Fig. 1
a, the CYS-CDs particles were uniform in size and well-dispersed. By selecting 50 samples under low magnification, a particle size distribution diagram was obtained, revealing an average particle size of 4.34 nm. Fig. 1 b demonstrates that the carbon dots posses good crystallinity, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [8].

As shown in Fig. 1 c, the surface functional groups of CYS-CDs were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The broad absorption band observed between 3260 and 3600 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H/N–H bonds. The characteristic peaks at 2851 cm−1 and 1401 cm−1 show the existence of stretching vibration of C–H and C–N bonds, respectively. The peak at 1640 cm−1 corresponds to the absorption band of C=O bonds, while the peak at 1040 cm−1 corresponds to the tensile vibration of C–O [38].

The chemical constituents of CYS-CDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1 d, the XPS spectra of CYS-CDs revealed C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p, with their respective element percentages being 61.08 %, 18.58 %, 19.31 %, and 1.04 %. Fig. 1 e-h show the high resolution XPS spectra for C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and S 2p. The C1s fine spectrum can be deconvolved into three peaks at 284.0 eV, 284.7 eV and 286.7 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–O/C–N and C=O respectively. The N 1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 398.2 eV, 399.7 eV and 400.6 eV, which corresponding to pyridine N, N–H and graphitic N respectively[39]; For the O 1s spectrum, C=O and C–O can be observed at 529.7 eV and 530.6 eV; In the high resolution S 2p spectrum, the peaks at 163.0 eV and 164.2 eV correspond to S 2p2/3 and S 2p1/3, respectively[40].

In summary, the results obtained from XPS and FT-IR are in good agreement with each other. When combined with the TEM analysis, it can be concluded that CYS-CDs are monodisperse spherical nanoparticles with good crystallization. Furthermore, these nanoparticles contain hydrophilic groups such as –OH, −NH2 and –COOH on their surface.

The optical properties of CYS-CDs were investigated by UV–Vis absorption, excitation and emission spectrum. The absorption peak appeared at 332 nm, while the optimal excitation wavelength was at 355 nm, and the emission peak at 463 nm (Fig. 2
a). The optical photos of CYS-CDs illustrated that CYS-CDs appeared light yellow and almost colorless under daylight but exhibited bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light.

Fig. 2 b demonstrated the excitation wavelength dependence of CYS-CDs. As the excitation wavelength changed from 305 to 405 nm, the emission peak always remained at about 460 nm, showing good excitation wavelength independence. This suggests that CYS-CDs have only one emission center. The absolute quantum yield of CYS-CDs was measured to be 17.38 %. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime (τ CYS-CDs) of CYS-CDs aqueous solution at 463 nm was determined to be 4.83 ns using three-exponential fitting (Fig. 2 c). The optical stability of CYS-CDs was confirmed by continuous ultraviolet irradiation ( Fig. S2 ). Furthermore, CYS-CDs maintained good fluorescence performance across a wide pH range, except for a few strong acids or alkalis ( Fig. S3 ).

CYS-CDs was tested by circular dichroism to analyze its optical rotation and chirality. As shown in Fig. 2 d, CYS-CDs showed positive characteristic peaks in the wavelength range of 190–230 nm, confirming its chiral characteristics. It is noteworthy that the chiral source D-Cys showed a strong negative characteristic peak signal at 209 nm. The completely opposite characteristic peak signal of CYS-CDs at 202 nm confirmed that their chirality came from the inversion of the chiral signal of D-Cys, which may be attributed to the high temperature conditions during the hydrothermal process [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific response of CYS-CDs to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para:
Through chiral molecule and ion selectivity experiments, the fluorescence response of CYS-CDs to common chiral molecules (D-aspartic acid, D-isoascorbic acid, D-lysine, D-tryptophan, dopamine, glutathione, L-ascorbic acid, L-arginine, L-histidine, L-lysine, L-methionine, L-serine, L-threonine, L-tryptophan, L-valine) was explored. As shown in Fig. 3
a, CYS-CDs only showed a very weak fluorescence enhancement to L-Lys, and had almost no response to other chiral molecules tested, with no obvious specific recognition phenomenon.

As shown in Fig. 3 b, there are more than ten common ions (Ca2+, Cd2+, ClO−, Co2+, CO3 2–, Cu2+, F−, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, I−, K+, Na+, Cl−, PO4 3−, SCN−, Sn4+), CYS-CDs have completely different specific fluorescence responses to Sn4+ and Cu2+ at the same time, showing significant fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence quenching respectively. In this regard, the sensitivity response effect of CYS-CDs to two ions was explored through fluorescence titration experiments. As shown in Fig. 3 c, as the Sn4+ concentration increases from 0 µM to 160 µM, the fluorescence intensity of CYS-CDs gradually increases to 128 % of the initial fluorescence intensity. Continue to increase the Sn4+ concentration to 200 µM, and CYS-CDs basically no longer show significant fluorescence enhancement phenomenon. Since there is no good linear or exponential fitting relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CYS-CDs and the Sn4+ concentration ( Fig. 3 d), which cannot meet the ideal Sn4+ detection requirements for practical applications, no in-depth exploration was carried out in the follow-up. For Cu2+ (Fig. 3 e, f), as its concentration increases from 0 µM to 400 µM, the fluorescence of CYS-CDs gradually weakens to 50 % of the initial fluorescence intensity; and in the range of 40–360 µM, there is a good linear fitting relationship between (F0-F) and the concentration of Cu2+ (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of CYS-CDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+ respectively), consistent with the regression equation y = 232.49 x-12117.00, R2 = 0.9891, and the calculated detection limit is 8.39 µM. Therefore, CYS-CDs are expected to become fluorescent sensors for Cu2+.

Due to the fact that the concentration of 360 μM Cu2+ is the lowest concentration that maximizes the fluorescence intensity of CYS-CDs, CYS-CDs and 360 µM Cu2+ were selected to construct a fluorescence probe CYS-CDs + Cu2+ for subsequent exploration of chiral recognition applications.

As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence response of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ to lysine enantiomers was explored. With increasing L-Lys concentration (0–520 µM), the fluorescence intensity of the probe gradually increased, reaching 205 % of the initial intensity. A blue shift in emission wavelength was observed. A good linear relationship exists between the fluorescence F-F0 and lysine concentration (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ in the presence and absence of L-Lys, respectively), described by the equation y = 142.20 x + 5034.17, R2 = 0.9825. The detection range is 0–520 µM, with a calculated detection limit of 13.70 µM. In contrast, the fluorescence of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ remained almost unchanged with increasing D-Lys concentration (0–520 µM). Therefore, the fluorescence probe CYS-CDs + Cu2+ can quantitatively detect L-Lys and effectively distinguish it from D-Lys.

To determine whether CYS-CDs play a major role in the chiral recognition of lysine enantiomers, the fluorescence spectrum of CYS-CDs was investigated in the absence of Cu2+ across varying concentrations of L-Lys/D-Lys. As shown in Fig. 5
a, upon increasing the L-Lys concentration from 0 to 520 µM, CYS-CDs showed fluorescence changes similar to those observed with CYS-CDs + Cu2+, specifically, an enhancement in fluorescence accompanied by a slight blue shift in emission. Within this concentration range, a strong linear correlation was found between the fluorescence intensity (F’-F0′) and L-Lys concentration (F’ and F0′ are the fluorescence intensities of CYS-CDs in the presence and absence of L-Lys, respectively), adhering to the linear equation y = 93.47 x + 1260.32, R2 = 0.9925. However, the fluorescence intensity was only enhanced to about 130 % of its initial value.

This slight change could potentially introduce significant errors in the quantitative detection of L-Lys. As the concentration of D-Lys increased from 0 µM to 520 µM, CYS-CDs fluorescence decreased by 13 %, showing a weaker change compared to L-Lys. No good linear relationship (Fig. 5 d) was observed between fluorescence intensity and D-Lys concentration. In summary, although CYS-CDs cannot accurately perform quantitative detection of L-Lys or D-Lys, it is undoubtedly possible to distinguish lysine enantiomers based on fluorescence response differences, indicating its key role in chiral recognition. Therefore, it can be concluded that when using CYS-CDs + Cu2+ fluorescent probe to distinguish lysine enantiomers, CYS-CDs is the key to chiral recognition, while Cu2+ enhances the fluorescence response.

In addition, the effect of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ on the enantiomeric excess percentage (ee%) of lysine was explored in this work, which signifies the percentage of L-Lys excess relative to D-Lys in their total concentration. The ee% is calculated using the formula ee%=([R]-[S]/[R] + [S])*100 %, where [R] and [S] represent the concentrations of the two enantiomeric configuration, respectively [42]. With reference to the quantitative measurement range of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ for L-Lys, the sum of [R] and [S] was kept at a constant value of 500 µM for spectral testing (Table 1 ).

As shown in Fig. 6
a, as ee% gradually changes from 100 % to −100 %, the fluorescence intensity drops and the emission wavelength redshifts. The corresponding relationship between fluorescence intensity peak and enantiomeric excess percentage of each group of samples is shown in Fig. 6 b. The two exhibit a good linear fitting relationship, satisfying the regression equation y = −74.93x + 119306.91, R2 = 0.9857. Therefore, it can be considered that the fluorescence probe can be used to detect the enantiomeric excess percentage of lysine, and it is expected to be applicable for detecting the purity of a specific lysine configuration. Undeniably, this application in the detection of ee% of lysine in real samples is worth further exploration.

Considering that L-Cys is cheaper and more readily available than D-Cys, this work further explored the fluorescence response of L-CYS-CDs to lysine enantiomers (Fig. S4a–h). L-CYS-CDs exhibit weak fluorescence changes when distinguishing L-Lys and D-Lys, limiting practical application. Furthermore, regardless of the solution concentration, Cu2+ does not enable L-CYS-CDs + Cu2+ to show a consistent fluorescence response to either L-Lys or D-Lys. Therefore, in constructing the fluorescent probe CYS-CDs + Cu2+, D-Cys is the most effective chiral source for generating the desired response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ in paper-based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the effect of fluorescent probe CYS-CDs + Cu2+ on directly detecting L-Cys by test paper was further explored. All the paper utilized in the experiment is qualitative filter paper, which adheres to the standard code GB/T1914-2017, and is manufactured by Hangzhou Special Paper Co., Ltd. Cutting the qualitative filter paper sheets to the same size, and dropping CYS-CDs and CYS-CDs + Cu2+ solution into the paper sheets in groups to fully soak them for pretreatment. After completely drying, dropped L-Lys solutions with different concentrations into the test paper, and observed the color difference after drying. As shown in Fig. 7 , samples 1–2 respectively represent blank pieces of paper treated with CYS-CDs and CYS-CDs + Cu2+ solution, and 3–6 respectively represent pieces of paper treated with CYS-CDs + Cu2+ and dripped with 50, 100, 150 and 200 mL of CYS solution. As shown in Fig. 7a, after drying, the samples treated with different solutions almost all show the primary colors of paper, and it is difficult to distinguish the differences with naked eyes. Under ultraviolet light, the fluorescence quenching effect of Cu2+ on CYS-CDs can be clearly seen in comparison with samples 1 and 2, while in comparison with samples 3–6, with the increase of L-Lys concentration, the brightness of blue fluorescence of paper sheets can be gradually enhanced. In addition, the RGB values of the optical photos of the paper under ultraviolet light were extracted by the color picker tool in PowerPoint, and the change of RGB values of the three-color light mode is analyzed (Fig. 7b). The ratio of the redlight R values of the six groups of samples is 0, and the greenlight G values and blue light B values show the highest values in the first group, and the lowest values in the second group, in which the G values gradually increase from 2 to 6 groups, and the B values first increase and then remain unchanged. The magnitude of RGB values represents the intensity of brightness, so we can conclude that the brightness of groups 2–6 gradually increases from the histogram, which is completely consistent with the phenomenon observed by the naked eye. It is worth noting that, as shown in Fig. 7c, there is a certain linear law between the G value and the concentration of L-Lys, and they satisfy the linear regression equation y = 15.90 x + 85.88, R2 = 0.9909. This result once again confirms the reliability of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ paper-based sensor for L-Lys.

On the one hand, as shown in Fig. 8
a, by comparing the absorption spectrum of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ with that of Cu2+ and CYS-CDs, it is found that the addition of Cu2+ does not cause a new absorption peak of CYS-CDs + Cu2+, and its absorption spectrum has no obvious change, so it can be considered that Cu2+ does not cause the static quenching of CYS-CDs, and the surface of CYS-CDs. On the other hand, combined with Fig. 8 b, c, the fluorescence lifetime (τ CYS-CDs) of CYS-CDs is 4.83 ns, and after adding Cu2+, the fluorescence lifetime (τ CYS-CDs+Cu 2+) is 3.96 ns, τ CYS-CDs/τCYS-CDs+Cu 2+. To sum up, the fluorescence quenching of CYS-CDs in contact with Cu2+ is dynamic quenching, that is, Cu2+ collides with CYS-CDs in excited singlet state, so that CYS-CDs returns to the ground state in the form of non-radiative transition, thus fluorescence quenching occurs [43,44].

As shown in Fig. 8 d, the circular dichroisms of CYS-CDs, CYS-CDs + Cu2+, CYS-CDs + Cu2++L-Lys and CYS-CDs + Cu2++D-Lys are compared respectively. Firstly, the circular dichroism of CYS-CDs + Cu2+ showed that the chiral signal of CYS-CDs decreased rapidly, and almost no obvious characteristic peak was observed. The addition of Cu2+ not only quenched the fluorescence, but also affected the chiral signal of CYS-CDS. The chiral characteristic signal of CYS-CDs + Cu2++L-Lys is positive, which is obviously enhanced compared with CYS-CDs + Cu2+, and the characteristic peak appears at 208 nm, which is consistent with the positive signal of CYS-CDs. On the contrary, the chiral characteristic signal of CYS-CDs + Cu2++D-Lys is negative, and the position of the characteristic peak is 201 nm, which is opposite to that of CYS-CDs and the position of the characteristic peak shifts. Therefore, it can be inferred that the process of chiral recognition is: the photoluminescence activity and circular dichroism of CYS-CDs are both affected during the collision between Cu2+ and the surface of CYS-CDs, and the addition of L-Lys may hinder the interaction between Cu2+ and CYS-CDs to some extent, weaken the change of its surface state, thus leading to the phenomenon of fluorescence and chiral signal recovery [45,46]. CYS-CDs + Cu2++L-Lys. On the contrary, the opposite chiral signal of CYS-CDs + Cu2++D-Lys and CYS-CDs, that is, the shift of characteristic peak, cannot make the probe respond to D-Lys obviously. The signal difference of circular dichroism explains the significant difference of chiral fluorescent probes in the choice of amino acid enantiomers.

In this work, a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis was adopted as the experimental methods, which not only ensured the strong fluorescence of CYS-CDs after high temperature carbonization under appropriate reaction conditions, but also avoided the complete destruction of chirality of CYS-CDs at too high temperature. Circular dichroism has confirmed that CYS-CDs are chiral carbon points. However, it is still difficult for single-component CYS-CDs to directly recognize common chiral molecules, and it can only show regular fluorescence response to Cu2+ within a certain concentration range. The quenching of CYS-CDs by Cu2+ was proven to be dynamic, caused by surface collision. On this basis, the CYS-CDs + Cu2+ chiral fluorescence probe was successfully constructed, and the chiral distinction between L-Lys and D-Lys, the quantitative detection of L-Lys, and the enantiomer excess detection of L-Lys in the lysine enantiomer mixture could be realized through the obvious difference in fluorescence intensity. The mechanism of the probe's differentiation between L-Lys and D-Lys can be directly explained by the signal changes of circular dichroism. By comparing the difference of CYS-CDs and L-CYS-CDs in lysine enantiomer recognition, it was found that L-CYS-CDs cannot recognize lysine enantiomer well, only CYS-CDs can produce significant enantiomer recognition effect. Finally, the environmentally friendly and intuitive paper-based sensor provides much practicability for the application of CYS-CDs in chemical and biological sensing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020YQ33).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126143.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
